design_philosophy.core_loop: Real-time party-based combat with pause-menu strategy layer; explore overworld across three time periods (past, present, future) to gather allies and unlock story progression
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Vengeance-driven narrative (protagonist Cress seeking to prevent destruction of his hometown) intersects with time-travel mechanics that create temporal paradoxes and moral ambiguity around protagonist Dhaos
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Command a growing ensemble of diverse party members through an epic, time-spanning adventure with high narrative stakes; balance real-time action reflexes against tactical party management
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Linear Motion Battle System (LMBS) enforces asymmetric player agency: only main character is directly controllable in real-time; AI-controlled allies operate on modes (passive/active) or require pause-menu intervention. This creates tension between action-game mastery and RPG party coordination. Time-travel narrative structure gates character availability and world state by era, forcing specific recruitment sequences.

difficulty.checkpointing: Story progression gates checkpoints; can return to previous time periods via time-travel mechanic
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Pause-menu battle system allows player to override AI behavior and manually direct party spells/items, reducing difficulty spikes. Time period selection provides implicit difficulty scaling (past generally easier than present/future). No explicit difficulty settings documented.

progression.upgrade_system: Spirit summoning system: Claus F. Lester collects magical rings and summons spirits after defeating them; characters learn spells/techniques through leveling. Equipment upgrades available via exploration and story progression.
progression.unlock_mechanism: Time-travel gates character recruitment and world access. Story progresses through three temporal eras (past → present → future); each period unlocks new areas, allies, and story revelations. Defeating Dhaos schemes in one era influences subsequent eras.
replayability_hooks: Multiple playable character roster with different playstyles (swordsman, archer, priestess, summoner, half-elf mage, ninja child) encourages different party compositions
Spirit summoning completion incentivizes re-engagement for collectathon gameplay
Time-travel structure allows post-game revisiting of earlier eras for missed content

world_building_techniques: Time-travel narrative framing: protagonist moves between past, present, and future to understand root cause of conflict and gather allies across temporal layers
Character-driven ensemble storytelling: six playable characters with individual backstories integrated into main plot (e.g., Arche's discrimination due to half-elf heritage, Chester's family ties)

Voice acting as narrative anchor: first Super Famicom game with original vocalized theme song; later Full Voice Edition (2006 PSP remake) added full voice acting during story scenes, elevating narrative prominence
Environmental progression as narrative: world state visibly changes across time periods, reflecting consequences of player actions and story decisions

boss_design.philosophy: Dhaos serves as persistent antagonist across all three time periods; conflict is not merely mechanical but embedded in temporal narrative paradox. Boss encounters test both reflexes (LMBS real-time positioning) and resource management (party spell/item selection via pause menu).
notable_bosses: Dhaos (primary antagonist; multiple encounters across different eras with escalating difficulty and narrative context)

faction_indicators: Dhaos's war-mongering agenda frames conflict as ideological (freedom/conquest vs. peace/stasis)
Time-travel causality creates faction-like temporal allegiances: player must choose which era's allies to prioritize
danger_signals: Hometown destruction as inciting incident establishes stakes of loss and vengeance
War-mongering antagonist threatens multiple time periods simultaneously, escalating danger across narrative acts
environmental_storytelling: Three distinct time periods (past, present, future) with visual and mechanical differences reflecting world state changes
Destruction of Cress's hometown as environmental anchor for emotional investment and narrative motivation
Spirit summoning locations and defeated enemy remnants serve as environmental markers of player progress
